records.rationale: These are two distinct test cases covering different `ChainId` variants. The setup and assertion shape are the same, but the duplicated structure is small and intentional in test code to verify separate inputs and outcomes; it does not indicate an unhealthy repeated implementation pattern.

The fragments are structurally similar, but they assert different entries in the signal gate matrix: SpecAdr merge strictness versus CatalogSpec merge strictness. In tests, this small repetition is acceptable because each case documents a distinct expected behavior with a specific failure name.

These are two narrowly scoped test cases covering different enum variants (`ChainId::CatalogSpec` vs `ChainId::ImplCatalog`). The shared setup is minimal and the duplication is justified by distinct expected coverage rather than copy-pasted logic that should be abstracted.

These are two distinct matrix-cell tests, not a repeated implementation of the same behavior. The only shared structure is the standard test setup and assertion pattern; the differing `GateKind` values exercise different inputs and expected outcomes, so the duplication is intentional test coverage rather than a DRY problem.

These are two separate test cases covering different inputs and expected outcomes (`Commit` -> `Interim` vs `Merge` -> `Strict`). The shared setup is minimal and intentional for readability in a test matrix, so this is not a meaningful DRY violation.

These fragments are similar test scaffolds, but they validate different domain cases and expected outcomes: `ChainId::CatalogSpec` must resolve to `Strict`, while `ChainId::ImplCatalog` must resolve to `Interim`. Reusing the same setup pattern in separate tests is normal here and does not indicate harmful duplication.

These snippets are intentionally similar test cases, but they exercise different `ChainId` inputs and assert different enum values. The repetition is minimal and localized to a short unit test, so it does not create a meaningful DRY problem.

These are two separate test cases covering different `ChainId` variants (`SpecAdr` vs `AdrUser`) with the same shared setup and assertion shape. The repetition is limited to a small test pattern and serves distinct behavior coverage, so it is not a meaningful DRY violation.

The fragments repeat a very small test pattern, but they validate different entries in the signal gate matrix: `ChainId::AdrUser` versus `ChainId::ImplCatalog`. The duplication is localized, explicit, and in test code where separate named cases improve coverage readability without creating a shared maintenance hazard.

These are two small test cases that intentionally share the same setup/assertion structure but verify different inputs and outcomes (`Commit -> Interim` vs `Merge -> Strict`). The overlap is minimal and normal for unit tests, so this is not a meaningful DRY violation.

These fragments are intentionally similar test cases but they exercise different behavior: one asserts `SpecAdr` resolves `Strict` for `GateKind::Commit`, the other for `GateKind::Merge`. Repeating the setup in separate focused tests is appropriate here because each test documents a distinct rule and failure mode.

The fragments are two small, table-like unit tests that assert distinct matrix entries: SpecAdr merge strictness and ImplCatalog merge strictness. The repeated setup/assertion shape is intentional for per-chain coverage and keeps each expected gate behavior explicit; extracting this two-line pattern would not remove meaningful logic duplication.

These two fragments follow the same test pattern, but they exercise different inputs (`ChainId::SpecAdr` vs `ChainId::CatalogSpec`) and verify distinct cases. In test code, that level of repetition is usually acceptable because each assertion documents a separate behavior rather than duplicating implementation logic.

These fragments are two distinct test cases that exercise different inputs and expected outcomes (`ChainId::AdrUser` -> `Interim` versus `ChainId::SpecAdr` -> `Strict`). The shared setup is minimal and normal in table-style unit tests, so this does not indicate harmful duplication.

The two fragments cover different input combinations and expected outcomes for `ChainGateEntry::resolve`. The existing test verifies an interim commit gate with a strict merge gate, while the changed test verifies a strict commit gate with an interim merge gate. They exercise the same method, but they are not redundant copies of the same case.

The two fragments are similar test cases, but they exercise different input combinations and assertions. The repeated `ChainGateEntry` construction is a small, purposeful setup duplication in tests, not a harmful DRY violation.

These two fragments share the same test fixture setup, but they exercise different behavior: one verifies `resolve(GateKind::Merge)` and the other verifies `resolve(GateKind::Commit)`. Reusing the same literal values in nearby tests is normal and does not indicate a meaningful DRY problem here.

These are two distinct test cases that assert the same contract for different `ChainId`/`GateKind` combinations. The repeated setup is minimal and confined to tests, so this does not rise to a meaningful DRY violation.

These fragments show two different layer crates exposing similarly named modules (`dry_check`, `semantic_dup`, `spec`, `track`, etc.), but that is not DRY duplication by itself. The domain crate re-exports core types and ports, while the infrastructure crate exposes concrete adapters and codecs. The overlap is architectural naming alignment, not repeated implementation or duplicated logic.

The overlap here is structural, not duplicated implementation logic. `libs/domain/src/lib.rs` is a domain-layer barrel that re-exports domain types and modules, while `libs/usecase/src/lib.rs` is a separate use-case-layer barrel that re-exports selected domain identity types and declares its own use-case modules. The shared names reflect layer boundaries and composition, not copy-pasted behavior, so this does not constitute a DRY violation.

The duplicated fragment is a tiny test helper that constructs deterministic ContentHash values from a byte. It appears to be local test-support code, not production logic or domain behavior, and extracting it would add coupling between unrelated test modules for little maintainability benefit.

Both fragments are tiny local helper functions that construct deterministic `ContentHash` test values from a repeated byte. The duplication is trivial, self-contained, and likely test-scoped or fixture-like; extracting it into shared production/domain code would add coupling across domain and infrastructure boundaries without reducing meaningful maintenance risk.

The fragments are identical implementations, but they appear to be small local helper functions with different names in different crates/modules. A three-line test/data-construction helper that wraps `ContentHash::from_bytes([byte; 32])` does not by itself create a meaningful shared knowledge burden, and extracting it across domain/infrastructure boundaries would likely increase coupling for negligible benefit.

Both fragments are tiny deterministic `ContentHash` fixture helpers inside `#[cfg(test)]` test modules (`libs/domain/src/chain.rs` test module and `libs/usecase/src/catalogue_spec_refs.rs` test module). They do not duplicate production behavior or a shared domain rule; extracting them would create cross-module or cross-crate test coupling for a one-line fixture helper with no meaningful abstraction value.

The duplicated function is a tiny local test/data-construction helper: it deterministically creates a ContentHash filled with one byte. It carries no business rule or independent knowledge beyond the ContentHash constructor call, and extracting it would likely add coupling between unrelated module tests or force a shared test utility for a one-line convenience helper.

These helpers share the same high-level shape, but they are for different document types and use different constructors/inputs (`hash(0xcd)` versus `ts()` plus a fixed hash string). That is parallel test setup, not a meaningful DRY violation.

The two fragments overlap only at a very generic construction pattern for `CatalogueSpecSignalsDocument`. The changed code is a small parameterized test helper (`doc(signals)`) and the existing code is a fixed sample fixture with specific signal contents. They do not duplicate the same concrete data or business logic, so this is not a meaningful DRY violation.

The two fragments overlap only at a very small fixture-construction pattern: both build a `CatalogueSpecSignalsDocument` with a dummy hash. The surrounding logic differs materially (`Vec<CatalogueSpecSignal>` vs `Vec<(String, ConfidenceSignal)>`, and one helper also maps tuples into signals), and they live in different modules/layers. This is not enough duplication to count as a DRY violation on its own.

The two helpers share the same construction pattern, but they are not duplicating the same concrete setup: one builds a document from caller-provided signals and the other always returns an empty-signals sample document with a different fixed hash. This is small, local test scaffolding rather than repeated domain logic that should be centralized.

These two tests share the same harness pattern, but they exercise different behaviors and assertions: one verifies that all-blue signals produce no findings in both modes, while the other verifies that a red signal is always an error and names the offending entry. Reusing the same setup code in tests like this is normal and does not indicate harmful duplication.

These tests follow the same verification pattern, but they exercise different public APIs in different modules (`check_catalogue_spec_signals` vs `check_spec_doc_signals`) with different fixture builders and assertions. The repeated structure is ordinary test coverage duplication, not an avoidable DRY violation in the implementation.

The two functions share the same broad control-flow shape, but they are separate domain validations with different inputs, predicates, and error text. `check_catalogue_spec_signals` checks catalogue-specific grounding rules, while `check_type_signals` checks TDDD type declarations; the overlap is only a small, conventional filter-and-bucket pattern that is not enough on its own to justify a DRY violation.

The two tests share a small amount of setup and assertion structure, but they exercise different behaviors: yellow is only an error in strict mode, while red is an error in both interim and strict modes. This is ordinary test duplication for clarity, not a meaningful DRY violation.

These tests share a generic assertion pattern, but they exercise different public functions (`check_catalogue_spec_signals` vs `check_spec_doc_signals`) with different inputs and different expected message content. The overlap is limited to a standard test shape for error handling, which is normal and does not indicate harmful duplication.

The two tests share a generic assertion pattern, but they exercise different public APIs and different domain helpers/types. The overlap is limited to a small, readable test shape, and extracting a shared helper would likely obscure the intent of each test more than it would reduce meaningful duplication.

The two fragments share some test setup shape, but they exercise different behaviors and assert different outcomes: one verifies yellow is a warning in interim mode, the other verifies red is an error in both interim and strict modes. The repetition is limited to a small amount of test scaffolding and is not duplication of a single source of truth or logic that would create maintenance risk.

These tests share a small assertion pattern, but they exercise different public APIs (`check_catalogue_spec_signals` vs `check_spec_doc_signals`) with different input models and different expected warning content. The overlap is shallow and does not indicate harmful duplication or a maintainability problem in this context.

These tests share the same high-level pattern, but they exercise different public APIs in different layers (`check_catalogue_spec_signals` vs `check_type_signals`) with different fixtures, entity names, and assertion details. The overlap is limited to a common warning-path shape, which is normal in test code and does not indicate harmful duplication.

These fragments are similar test patterns, but they exercise different production code paths and different rules: one checks catalogue-spec red handling in interim and strict modes, the other checks type-signal yellow handling only in strict mode. The overlap is incidental test structure, not duplicated domain logic that should be factored out for DRY reasons.

The two snippets share the same high-level test pattern, but they exercise different functions, helpers, and domain types (`check_catalogue_spec_signals` vs `check_type_signals`). This is normal test duplication across separate units, not a maintainability problem that DRY is meant to prohibit.

The overlap is only at the module-registration level: both crates expose similarly named domain/infrastructure modules such as `dry_check`, `semantic_dup`, `spec`, `tddd`, and `verify`. That is not a DRY violation by itself because these layers serve different responsibilities and the files they point to are different implementations, not duplicated logic. The fragments do not show the same algorithm, validation, or data model being reimplemented in both places.

The overlap here is at the package boundary, not duplicated implementation. `libs/domain/src/lib.rs` centralizes domain types and re-exports, while `libs/usecase/src/lib.rs` declares use-case modules and intentionally re-exports a few domain identity types (`TrackId`, `LayerId`, `ValidationError`) to keep the CLI boundary typed without depending on `domain` directly. The shared names like `dry_check`, `guard`, `hook`, `review_v2`, and `track_phase` indicate layered modules, not copy-pasted logic or repeated business rules.

These two tests share a small amount of standard test setup, but they verify different failure modes (`ParseFailed` vs `SchemaVersionUnknown`) and are not redundant. The repetition is limited to a common pattern that keeps each case explicit and readable, so it does not rise to a DRY violation.

The two fragments are adjacent test cases that use the same assertion pattern, but they exercise different failure modes (`FileNotFound` vs `ParseFailed`). This is normal test duplication, not a meaningful DRY violation that would justify refactoring.

These fragments reuse the same test harness pattern, but they exercise different failure modes with different inputs and assertions. The repetition is small and intentional in test code, and it improves readability by keeping each error case explicit rather than abstracting away the scenario.

These two test cases are structurally similar, but they are validating different boundary inputs (`$schema_version: 0` vs `99`) and different expected error payloads. In test code, this level of repetition is usually acceptable when it keeps each case explicit and easy to read; it does not look like duplicated business logic or an extracted helper that would materially improve maintainability.

These snippets reuse a common test pattern, but they exercise different inputs and assert different error paths (`commit_gate` vs `commit_gate.impl_catalog`). The repeated setup is small and the duplication is incidental to maintaining clear, targeted tests rather than a meaningful DRY problem.

These are two small, focused test cases exercising different error paths. They share the same helper pattern (`write_config`, `load_signal_gates_config`, `assert!` with `matches!`), but that repetition is normal in tests and does not indicate a harmful DRY problem by itself. The JSON fixtures differ in the specific invalid condition being verified, so each case remains distinct and readable.

These snippets follow the same test pattern, but they exercise different failure modes and assert different error variants. The shared setup (`write_config`, `load_signal_gates_config`, `assert!(matches!(...))`) is small and idiomatic for adjacent unit tests, so this is not a meaningful DRY violation.

These snippets use the same simple test pattern, but they exercise different failure modes (`FileNotFound` vs `ParseFailed`) and the repetition is limited to standard test setup/assertion boilerplate. This is not the kind of duplicated logic a DRY violation would target.

These tests share only ordinary setup and assertion structure. They cover different failure modes and validate different error details: one checks malformed JSON, the other checks a valid non-object JSON document and the error path/reason. That is not redundant enough to be a DRY violation.

These fragments share a common test shape, but they exercise different inputs and different failure modes: one validates a non-string value and the other validates an unknown string. In test code, this level of repetition is acceptable when it keeps each case explicit and easy to reason about. It does not look like accidental duplication that should be factored out just to satisfy DRY.

These snippets share only a generic test pattern (set up a config path, call `load_signal_gates_config`, assert on the error). They exercise different failure modes and different error variants: one verifies `FileNotFound` for a missing file, the other verifies `ParseFailed` for non-object JSON and checks the error path/reason. That is normal test repetition, not a DRY violation.

These fragments use the same test harness pattern, but they exercise different failure modes and assert different error variants (`ParseFailed` versus `SchemaVersionUnknown`). Reusing a consistent arrange/act/assert shape in tests is normal and does not by itself indicate duplicated logic that should be abstracted.

Both fragments are tiny local test helpers used to construct CatalogueSpecSignal fixtures in separate modules/crates. Sharing this would add cross-module test utility coupling without removing meaningful domain knowledge duplication, so it does not constitute a DRY violation.

These fragments are not duplicating the same logic. The changed code in `libs/usecase/src/chain/spec_adr.rs` provides a concrete `check_freshness` implementation, while the code in `libs/domain/src/chain.rs` is only the trait signature plus documentation for the shared contract. A trait declaration and its implementation are expected to resemble each other; that is not DRY duplication.

The fragments are identical, but both are tiny private helpers inside separate test modules used to build deterministic ContentHash fixtures. This is acceptable test setup duplication rather than a production DRY violation; extracting it would add cross-module test coupling for no meaningful maintenance benefit.

The changed fragment is an implementation of the trait method declared in `libs/domain/src/chain.rs`, not a separate copy of business logic. The shared signature and comment are part of the abstraction boundary, and the implementation delegates to `check_catalogue_spec_signals(...)` rather than repeating the candidate code.

These fragments are not duplicating implementation logic. The domain file shows the trait contract for `evaluate_gate`, while the changed file provides the concrete implementation and delegates to `check_type_signals`. The shared signature and similar documentation are normal interface/implementation pairing, not a DRY violation.

The changed code is an implementation of the `evaluate_gate` contract declared in `libs/domain/src/chain.rs`, not a duplicated standalone copy of separate logic. The body simply delegates to `check_spec_doc_signals`, so there is no meaningful repeated implementation to refactor here.

The two fragments are not duplicating implementation logic; they are the generic domain trait contract and one chain-specific implementation plus its local documentation. The overlap is limited to the shared freshness concept and is expected because the concrete `spec_adr` implementation must specialize the generic `chain` interface described in `libs/domain/src/chain.rs`.

These helpers are structurally similar, but they are type-specific constructors in different layers (`ContentHash` vs `FragmentContentHash`) with different APIs. The repetition is limited to a tiny local wrapper and does not indicate a shared business rule that should be abstracted out.

The usecase crate is intentionally re-exporting a small set of domain identity and error types to present a typed boundary to callers. This is not duplicated business logic or repeated implementation; it is a deliberate API surface convenience and an architectural boundary decision. The candidate fragment in `domain` only defines and exports those types, while `usecase` forwards them for consumers, so there is no meaningful DRY violation here.

These fragments are not duplicating the same production logic in a way that creates a DRY problem. The changed code is a small helper that converts raw inputs into an `AdrRef`, while the candidate is a unit test asserting `AdrRef::new` works correctly. The overlap is only at the API usage level, which is expected and not a maintainability concern.

The fragments are structurally similar helper constructors, but they encode different local fixture details: different ID construction helpers and different ADR file/decision references. The overlap is mainly the required `SpecRequirement::new` call shape, not duplicated domain knowledge worth centralizing across these modules.

violation.refactor_proposal: Extract a shared AdrRef construction helper in an appropriate common test/support module or domain constructor, and replace both duplicated make_adr_ref definitions with calls to that shared helper.

records.rationale: The two fragments are identical implementations of the same helper, encoding the same AdrRef construction and unwrap-based anchor validation in two places. A future change to how ADR references are built would need to be duplicated across both files.

The fragments share the same constructor shape, but they encode different test fixture meanings: `req_blue` creates a requirement with a concrete ADR reference and constructs the id directly, while `req_red` creates an unreferenced requirement through the local `id` helper. This is small, local test-helper boilerplate rather than duplicated domain knowledge that would create a maintenance hazard.

The fragments are identical, but they are a tiny local test helper for constructing deterministic ContentHash values. Extracting this across crate/module boundaries would add more coupling and ceremony than the three-line duplication removes, so this is an acceptable test-code duplicate rather than an actionable DRY violation.

The fragments duplicate a small test/helper construction pattern, but they are not the same abstraction: the changed helper fixes the kind to "value_object", while the candidate helper accepts kind as a parameter in a different module. The repeated `TypeSignal::new(..., true, empty vectors...)` call is minor boilerplate around a constructor and does not by itself create a meaningful shared source of knowledge worth extracting across these modules.

The two fragments share a tiny helper shape, but they are not duplicating domain logic: each returns a different fixed timestamp for its own local test/support context. The difference in the literal makes them separate fixtures rather than a meaningful DRY breach.

These helpers are superficially similar, but they are not a meaningful DRY violation: each one exists to wrap a different domain type (`ContentHash` vs `FragmentContentHash`) and each calls a different constructor/API. The shared shape is only a tiny conversion wrapper, which is normal when keeping type-specific creation close to each module.

The two fragments do not duplicate the same implementation logic. They each define the public module surface for a different crate (`usecase` vs `infrastructure`), and the overlap is limited to normal layer wiring and naming consistency. That kind of parallel re-export/module declaration is architectural organization, not a DRY problem.

Both fragments are tiny test-local helper functions inside separate #[cfg(test)] modules. They encode a simple fixture-construction idiom rather than duplicated production behavior or domain logic, and extracting a shared helper across usecase and infrastructure test modules would add coupling for negligible maintenance benefit.

The two fragments are tiny private helper constructors with identical mechanics, but they live in different crates/modules (`usecase` and `infrastructure`) and likely exist only to keep local test/setup code readable. Extracting a shared helper would either introduce an inappropriate dependency boundary between layers or create a test/support abstraction for a two-line expression, which would add coupling without reducing meaningful knowledge duplication.

The fragments are mechanically identical, but this is a tiny local helper for constructing deterministic ContentHash values from a byte. It does not encode separate business knowledge, and extracting it would likely add cross-module coupling between usecase and infrastructure code for negligible benefit.

Both fragments are identical, but they are tiny test-local helper functions inside separate `#[cfg(test)]` modules. The duplication is incidental test setup, has no production behavior, and sharing it would likely introduce unnecessary coupling between independent test modules for a one-line `ContentHash` fixture constructor.

The two fragments are complementary, not redundant: the helper `_assert_chain_bounds<T>()` centralizes the trait-bound check, and the test invokes it with `AdrUserChain` to trigger a compile-time validation. The test body does not repeat the bound logic itself, so this is not a DRY violation.

These snippets repeat only a small test pattern, but they are checking different concrete chain types against different bound helpers (`AdrUserChain` with `_assert_chain_bounds` vs `SpecAdrChain` with `_assert_persisted_chain_bounds`). That is intentional, type-specific test boilerplate rather than an avoidable duplication of business logic.

The two fragments share the same test pattern, but they cover different behavior: one asserts that a red report is an error in both interim and strict modes, while the other asserts that a yellow report is only an error in strict mode. This is test similarity, not harmful duplication, because the assertions target distinct invariants and are not repeated without adding coverage.

These helpers are structurally similar, but they are not redundant: each asserts a different compile-time contract for a different chain family. The duplicated shape is intentional boilerplate for trait-bound verification, not repeated business logic.

These fragments use the same compile-time assertion pattern, but they enforce different type constraints: one checks `LiveSoTChain` and the other checks `PersistedSoTChain`. The repetition is limited to a tiny test-only helper whose purpose is to pin distinct trait bounds, so it is intentional duplication rather than an avoidable DRY problem.

These are the same compile-time assertion pattern, but they serve different trait combinations in different modules: `PersistedSoTChain` in `spec_adr.rs` and `LiveSoTChain` in `adr_user.rs`. The duplication is minimal, intentional, and specific to each invariant, so there is no meaningful shared behavior to extract without reducing clarity.

These snippets reuse the same placeholder scaffold, but they are for two distinct chains with different domain names, error types, file names, and ticket references (`T007` vs `T008`). The overlap is limited to a small, conventional stub pattern and does not indicate harmful duplication or a maintainability problem.

These functions share the same small freshness-check shape, but the duplicated part is just the local compare-and-error boilerplate around different domain types and accessors. Each chain needs its own stored/current extraction and stale-error payload, so this is reasonable repetition rather than a DRY problem.

These snippets share a small structural pattern, but they are not duplicating the same knowledge or behavior. One freshness check compares catalogue declaration hashes and returns `CatalogSpecStaleError`; the other derives signal counts and handles the optional cached value with `SpecAdrStaleError`. The differences are domain-specific and justify keeping the logic local rather than forcing a shared abstraction.

The overlap is limited to documentation and commentary describing the same chain behavior from two different levels (module-level overview and trait-impl API docs). There is no duplicated executable logic, no repeated computation, and no separate source of truth that could drift at runtime. This is explanatory repetition, not a DRY violation.

The two fragments share the same trait-implementation skeleton, but that repetition is intentional boilerplate for two different chain types with different associated types and documentation. The duplicated structure is small, necessary for type-specific behavior, and does not indicate copied logic that should be factored out.

The overlap is limited to parallel documentation and type-level boilerplate for two different chain implementations. It does not duplicate executable logic or create a maintenance hazard by itself; the wording is intentionally consistent because both modules follow the same persisted-chain pattern with different concrete types and filenames.

The two fragments are intentionally parallel trait implementations, not accidental duplication. They share the same `PersistedSoTChain` boilerplate because each chain needs the same trait-shaped metadata, but they differ in the concrete persisted type, error types, and domain-specific documentation. That kind of repetition is expected and keeps each chain explicit rather than hiding distinct behavior behind abstraction.

The two fragments share the same small adapter shape, but they encode different chain stages (catalog-spec vs impl-catalog) and different error types in separate verification implementations. The duplicated body is only a three-line formatting wrapper around stage-specific text, and extracting it would likely obscure the local stage label rather than remove meaningful domain knowledge duplication.

These snippets share the same small helper shape, but the differing chain identifiers and error-context strings are part of each module's domain-specific output. This is intentional local repetition, not a DRY problem that would benefit from abstraction.

These two tests share the same arrange/act structure, but they exercise different outcomes: one verifies the gate passes on a blue signal, the other verifies it fails on a red signal. That is deliberate test coverage duplication, not an avoidable DRY violation.

These tests share a small setup pattern, but they validate different outcomes and assertions (yellow yields a warning, red yields an error). The repetition is minimal and serves clarity rather than redundant implementation logic.

The two snippets reuse the same test pattern, but they are validating different chains (`CatalogSpecChain` vs `ImplCatalogChain`) with different helpers and fixtures. This is ordinary parallel test coverage for analogous behavior, not a harmful DRY duplication.

These are parallel tests for two different chains with different domain helpers and assertions. The shared structure is a normal test pattern, not harmful repetition, because each test verifies a distinct public behavior and the duplicated shape is small and intentional.

The fragments share a small, structural trait-implementation pattern, but each implementation binds a different chain type, ChainId, and associated Input type. This is expected boilerplate for implementing ChainIdentity per chain, not duplicated business logic or knowledge that would drift independently.

This is only a small, necessary trait-implementation pattern repeated for two different chain types. The differing `ID` and `Input` associated types make the duplication explicit and expected, and there is no shared logic being copied that would benefit from extraction.

These snippets share a small structural pattern, but they are not redundant enough to count as a DRY violation. The `load` stubs and freshness-check comments are domain-specific to different chain implementations (`ImplCatalog` vs `CatalogSpec`) and differ in both error types and semantics (`declaration_hash()` vs stored `catalogue_declaration_hash`). This looks like intentional parallel code rather than accidental duplication that should be abstracted.

These fragments are complementary, not redundant. The empty generic helper encodes a trait-bound assertion, and the test calls it to enforce the bounds at compile time. The repeated names are part of the same assertion pattern, not duplicated business logic.

These fragments are only repeating the same one-line assertion pattern for two different concrete chain types. The shared logic is already factored into `_assert_persisted_chain_bounds`, so the remaining duplication is just type-specific test coverage and does not meaningfully violate DRY.
The changed fragment is a single test that reuses a shared assertion helper from the candidate fragment. This is intentional abstraction, not duplicated logic, so there is no DRY violation here.

These fragments repeat the same trait-bound assertion pattern, but the repetition is intentional and type-specific. Each test independently verifies that a different chain type satisfies the required bounds, so this is ordinary test coverage boilerplate rather than a DRY problem.

These fragments are not duplicating logic in a way that violates DRY. The changed test simply invokes the shared helper `_assert_persisted_chain_bounds::<CatalogSpecChain>()`, while the existing code defines that helper and centralizes the trait-bound check. This is reuse, not repetition.

The two fragments are structurally similar because they implement adjacent chain modules with the same framework pattern, but they are not duplicating a shared algorithm or business rule. The differences are material: different chain identities, different persisted document types, different filenames, and different freshness checks. This looks like intentional parallel boilerplate rather than a DRY violation.

These tests are structurally similar, but they verify the same contract on two different concrete chain implementations (`CatalogSpecChain` vs `ImplCatalogChain`) with different error types and expected messages. That is parallel coverage for distinct code paths, not unnecessary repetition in a way that would harm maintainability or indicate a DRY problem.

These are small, localized test cases that verify the same behavior for two different chain types. The overlap is intentional and limited to a few lines; each test is still tied to its own concrete type (`CatalogSpecChain` vs `SpecAdrChain`) and its own expected chain marker (`②` vs `①`). This is not the kind of duplication that creates maintenance risk or should be treated as a DRY violation.

These fragments are adjacent test cases that intentionally share setup while asserting different outcomes for `check_freshness`. The repeated scaffolding is small, improves readability, and does not indicate harmful duplication that should be abstracted away.

These tests share a small amount of setup shape, but they are verifying different behaviors in different chains: one checks the success path when hashes match, the other checks the stale-error path when hashes differ. The overlap is incidental and not enough to constitute harmful duplication.

The two fragments are structurally similar, but they are testing two different chain implementations (`CatalogSpecChain` vs `ImplCatalogChain`) with different input and helper types. This is parallel test coverage for comparable behavior, not unnecessary duplication inside a single abstraction boundary. The repeated assertion pattern is small and intentional.

These two helpers are structurally similar, but they are thin test-local constructor wrappers for different document types in different modules. The only shared shape is a trivial `new(...)` call, and the signatures are not interchangeable because one takes a `ContentHash` plus `CatalogueSpecSignal`, while the other takes `&str` plus `TypeSignal` and injects `ts()`. That is normal, low-value duplication rather than a maintainability problem.

The two fragments are identical, but this is a tiny private helper that creates a deterministic ContentHash from a byte value. By itself, duplicating a three-line test-style fixture helper across separate modules does not create meaningful knowledge duplication or maintenance risk; extracting it would likely add coupling for little value.

These tests are intentionally parallel, not redundant in a harmful DRY sense. Each fragment exercises a different concrete chain type and error type, and the only overlap is the shared assertion pattern for the same public behavior. That kind of mirrored test coverage is acceptable when it keeps the two implementations independently validated and the expected output differs (`chain ②` vs `chain ③`, different error constructors).

The two tests share the same high-level assertion pattern, but they exercise different domain types and expected outputs (`chain ②` vs `chain ①`, different stale-error payload shapes). This is small, localized test duplication that keeps each module's behavior explicit rather than an avoidable DRY problem.

The fragments repeat a small, intentional chain-module pattern: each implementation maps its chain-specific stale error into a `VerifyOutcome` with a distinct chain label, then starts a similarly shaped test module with chain-specific imports and types. The duplicated structure is boilerplate from the shared trait/verification pattern, while the concrete message, domain signals, document types, input type, and stale error type differ. Extracting this would likely reduce clarity or require an abstraction over very small code that is already explicit and chain-specific.

These fragments reuse the same tiny formatting pattern, but the duplication is narrow and intentionally scoped to two different chain implementations. The only behavioral difference is the chain-specific label, so extracting a shared helper would add indirection without materially reducing maintenance risk.

These tests are similar in intent, but they exercise different concrete types and setup helpers (`CatalogSpecChain` vs `ImplCatalogChain`, different input constructors, different document builders). The shared assertion is a small, expected pattern in parallel tests, not an avoidable duplication that meaningfully harms maintainability.

The two fragments exercise the same general behavior area, but they are not redundant copies of the same test. One verifies the success path when hashes match, while the other verifies the error path when hashes differ and asserts different outputs and error fields.
